what_it_does: Documents the one-way sync relationship between the external canonical design corpus and the in-repo read-copy, and describes the dual-mode lint behavior of design/_system/check.py.
observations: design/ is declared a one-way synced read-copy whose source of truth is build-prep/design/.
Hand-editing the in-repo copy is forbidden because changes are silently overwritten on the next sync.
Visual or address changes must be made in build-prep/design/_system/tokens.json or addresses.json, then emit.py/parse.py is run, then the result is re-synced.
design-system.css is generated and carries a DO NOT hand-edit header.
design/CLAUDE.md is referenced as the full corpus-keeper charter.
check.py has a default mockup-scan mode and an additive design-lint mode for gating live app source.
The design-lint exits non-zero for hardcoded off-token literals such as raw hex/rgba or +px values when --include-px is set.
Bespoke-element detection is a documented stub for C0 that returns [] and is wired into --fail-on but produces no findings today.
standouts: The canonical design corpus lives outside the repo and the in-repo directory is treated as a read-only sync target.
check.py serves both mockup coherence scanning and CI-style application source linting.
A lint detector is intentionally shipped as a non-functioning stub while remaining part of the failure path.
conventions: Marks generated or synced artifacts with explicit do-not-hand-edit warnings.
Externalizes the authoritative source of truth to a non-repo path.
Adds new functionality as additive modes rather than replacing existing behavior.
References capability markers like C0, F1, F4, F9 without defining them in this file.
README acts as a guardrail rather than a design specification.
concerns: The sync is asserted but no trigger, schedule, or verification mechanism is shown.
The Windows-side canonical path is environment-specific and absolute.
Because bespoke-element detection is a stub, --fail-on cannot actually fail on bespoke elements today, which could be misunderstood if the note is missed.
The file warns against hand-edits but provides no in-file enforcement.
notes: A guardrail README that tells readers the in-repo design corpus is a one-way synced read-copy and points them to the external canonical edit points and the design-lint gate.
questions: What process or tool performs the one-way sync and how is it triggered?
Where is build-prep/design/ located relative to the repository root?
What do the markers C0, F1, F4, and F9 represent?
How does check.py decide whether a CSS value is an allowed token variable or a forbidden literal?
What is the exact division of content between this README and design/CLAUDE.md?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches content: it is a short sync-direction note that also explains the design-lint role of check.py.
apparent_themes: Separation of authoring workspace from repository consumption
intention_signals: Prevent direct edits to the in-repo design corpus, force all token and address changes through the external canonical workspace, and provide an automated gate that keeps application code aligned with the design corpus.
novelty: The design corpus is authored and maintained outside the repository, with the in-repo copy treated strictly as a one-way-synced read-only artifact rather than a living design directory.
contribution: It records the sync boundary and edit discipline for the design corpus, and it documents the dual-mode lint contract of check.py that connects mockup coherence checks to application-source gating.
summary_for_embedding: company/design/README.md documents that the in-repo design corpus is a one-way synced read-copy of an external source and describes the default mockup-scan and additive design-lint modes of _system/check.py.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to enforce a single-source-of-truth workflow for design tokens and to direct users to the external edit points and automated lint gates.
